author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: The best YouTuber for FreeCAD I have found on the internet is Mango Jelly Solutions. He will teach you from start to finish. I've been following him for several years and still do. But as it was bound to happen, I graduated to Blender. FreeCAD is great for devices. Blender is great for free style modeling. FreeCAD just can't handle the meshes I'm creating now. And one more thing about FreeCAD: It probably has a work bench for what you are doing.

author: @claudiopiccoliromera2646
text: I still remember when I was a trainee and marveled at the upload process - one had to plug a programming portable computer to the CNC unit, in order to upload the programs to its memory; some time later, big improvement - floppy disks! And some years later, finally they got to be all networked. To be able to have your own CNC mill and be able to operate from any device would be far beyond my wildest dreams, back then.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: @ThinkingandTinkering now my suggestion since you are so familiar with 3D printing, maybe a good video on how to use slicer software and something like Tinkercad to make a negative of a part (ie you make the raw material then cut a cavity the shape of your part so you have a block with a hole that flipping the Z axis to a negative position will allow the mill to work its way down removing the block of material from around the part. reason I say this is because almost all 3D printers still use G-code for motion, the only thing you might have to do by hand is whip out notepad++ and load the G-code in so you can convert the portions for the motor of the extruder to blank spaces and insert the motor kick on and off code at the beginning and end. (*notepad++ is quite good at doing this and colorcoding all the possible stuff plus providing each line of text with a number for finding issues, in case you were not familiar with it :)  )

author: @FuhrChris
text:
For your first projects you may want to keep with "Tinker Cad" and simply out source the object to someone who knows C.A.D./C.A.M.? 
If you find you like C.A.D./C.A.M. then when you feel that the number of projects you WILL be doing is up to the learning.
(You may want to have someone else review you first attempts at CAD/CAM's? This is suggested in order to protect the machine.)

My father
A Boeing Draft'sman said that the very first CAD/CAM's had such low memory in the computer that holes to be drilled into the Aluminum were short cut as Hexagons' since that tool less memory in the computer.
The result was the very first CAM took a long time and went through a lot of tiny cutters.
What it out put was a piece of metal that Hexigons where it was supposed to just load and use a cheap drill bit.  Since the 1" holes had 1" on each flat surface the corners were larger than 1" and the piece could not be simply thrown into a drill press without there being 6 little precise made by expensive tiny rotating bits corners.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It's good advice mate and I really appreciate it - but to be honest I am also stubborn and don't mind a few cuts and bruises lol - but certainly if you are worried by it - that is a brilliant way to go - cheers

author: @FuhrChris
text:
You probably will want several bins that the dust collection system should feed into.
By simply changing the bins, from Aluminum, to brass, to steels, to everything else, you set yourself up for re-cycling, without having to sort through the materials.
Since I have a metal melter, instead of separated for going to the Re-cycling center I can melt some of the metal types back into usable objects.
Wood sawdust/powder can be an additive to another material, or pressed and a small amount of glue added and you have a fire starter for the fire place.
Clean PVC can be fed back into a hand held Plastic's welder.

I suspect that you can do a video about using the CNC machine (Post scanned) to clean up 3D prints.
And you already know about re-cycling 3D prints of material's like PLA.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - never occurred to me - this is why i love the comments - so many good ideas - cheers

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Hi Robert, great video. I considered making one some day. For CNC machines stepper motors, even with feedback is a bad idea. All kind of bad things happen if stepper motor's block and they will when torque is to high. Whatever happens, steppermotor should never lag from the pattern which they are driven.Servo motors are needed in such a case.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
Absolutely agree. For the most part servos with additional sensors beat steppers hands down when the forces encountered do unexpected things.
Each has pros and cons, but...

author: @FuhrChris
text:
I worked at Boeing's floor panel shop.
Composite floor panels were CNC-ed.
1. consider other materials and placing them on a Styrofoam bed that you can pernitrate the material making a complete cut through.
2. Watch not just your change out's from hogging over to final cuts.  (It's common for beginners' to think they can do everything with just one bit.)
3. I will always remember the day some Engineer (Over in another building) forgot to program the cutter raise and lower in the Z axis.  The CNC head just plowed through the base material (It needed repairs) but the Composite floor panel was ripped to shreds.  (What it was supposed to do was raise up move over then lower to drill the hole, then raise up move to the next spot and then lower down over and over.)

author: @jaedy1124
text:
Constraints are king. That's really all you need to know to get started with FreeCAD. Use constraints and use the spreadsheet to make your models parametric( responsive to variables). Once you learn those two things, the rest is absolutely easy.

I used SketchUp for years, even though I was working on Linux 98% of the time. I always had to have a Windows partition so I could use SketchUp, which was a pain. Since learning FreeCAD, it has absolutely changed the quality and complexity of my models!

author: @jamesross1003
text: Very interesting to me.  I like the CNC made things better than the 3D printed things because they are stronger and will hopefully last longer in harder materials.  Though admittedly, 3D printing has it's place and each different mode of creating things has it's end use as well as pros and cons.  The real trick is to use a variety of them together to make a complete and well made robust design.  Doable, yes it is but by no means easy.  At work places that I have been employed at I have been called the "Trade of all Jacks", rather than a "Jack of all trades".  LOL!  Not much of a compliment when you think about it, but it does have some implications that I feel are applicable when doing these kinds of projects.  Thanks for the video Robert!  Excited to see how this line of design plays out.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i believe the full quote is 'jack of all trades and master of none but often times better than master of one' - It really is a compliment when you know the whole quote mate - and I agree 100% with you - everything has it's place

author: @Ohm_Groan
text: I operate a 8' * 4' cnc for a set & prop building company near Manchester. I use Vectric VCarve Pro as an all in one CAD/CAM solution (the company pays for). One thing I would say is start with cheap (but not too cheap) cutting tools, and use an online speeds & feeds calculator as a starting reference point only, not as a final setting. Be mindful of the cutting sound and chip clearance to get dialed in. Once you have gotten used to that consider spending a little more on your cutting tools. If you're cutting the same material all the time, this shouldn't take too long to get a handle on, but if like me you're regularly switching materials, this can take a little longer (may be stating the obvious, but mention it just in case). All that said, it is a very rewarding skill, that to the curious can provide endless challenges (ahem! I mean learning opportunity). Above all, have fun.
